title: In lymphoma, where PET falls short, ctDNA delivers
text:

Small fragments of DNA can escape from cancer cells and enter the circulation; only the most sensitive tests can detect them.Credit: Jian Fan/ Getty ImagesAfter being treated for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), a negative readout from a PET scan should be good news for patients. The test tracks radiolabelled sugars as they’re broken down by the body’s most metabolically active tissues. Tumour cells tend to be very active, therefore the sugars will accumulate in malignant tissues and act like beacons signalling the tumour’s presence. A negative result should mean no more cancer.“That result seems as good as it can be," says Alex Herrera, chief of the Division of Lymphoma at City of Hope, Chicago. "But there's still a sizable chance that the disease will come back."Evidence suggests that, at the end of therapy, roughly 20% of patients with a negative PET result will relapse within three years. By contrast, around 50% of those with a positive PET result — indicating tumours are likely to regrow — remain cancer free1. Numbers like these emphasize that PET scans have too large a margin for error. "They're just not sensitive enough," Herrera adds.To improve survival rates, a more reliable test is needed, one that can sensitively and specifically detect the few cancer cells that remain after treatment ends, collectively referred to as minimal residual disease (MRD).Such tests are becoming available. Clinical and real-world data show that sensitive MRD detection can be achieved through circulating tumour DNA (ctDNA). These are small fragments of DNA released from tumour cells into the blood, carrying characteristic mutations that signal their malignant origins.Ultrasensitive MRD detection technology by Foresight Diagnostics (recently acquired by Natera) has been shown to detect ctDNA with a sensitivity that reaches below the parts per million. This test is available now for clinical trials and research use; a new version of Natera’s Signatera platform that incorporates Foresight’s technology will be available for clinical use in 2026. "It could be a game changer," says Herrera.The little things give cancer awayTo have achieved such sensitive MRD detection is no small feat, in part because ctDNA is so elusive. Even in patients with large tumours, ctDNA may be less than 10% of all cell-free DNA in the blood — most of which is released from healthy tissues2.“Early attempts at ctDNA detection weren't very predictive and certainly weren't better than PET,” explains Geoff Shouse, an assistant professor in the Department of Hematology & Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation at City of Hope.Shouse routinely sees patients with lymphoma and has paid close attention to the evolution of ctDNA detection techniques. “Newer tests have shown more significant promise,” he notes, “and it's mainly because they've found sequencing methods that improve sensitivity.”Initial efforts sought to identify ctDNA by the presence of individual variants, whereas the improved methods instead use more complex patterns. This is important because cell-free DNA can carry individual mutations that are not malignant in origin but nonetheless create biological noise, reducing the sensitivity of tests that define ctDNA using individual variations.Foresight Diagnostics’ PhasED-SeqTM technology achieves greater sensitivity by detecting more granular, tumour-informed patterns (see figure). These patterns are unique because they are formed by multiple variants within a single molecule of DNA — so-called phased variants3. The likelihood that two or more mutations co-occur by chance in a small DNA fragment is very low, which enables this approach to significantly reduce the background error rate that are often a challenge with traditional MRD approaches4.“The phased-variant approach allows you to achieve extremely high sensitivity, not just analytically, but clinically,” explains Ash Alizadeh, a Moghadam Family Professor of Medicine at Stanford University in the divisions of Oncology and Hematology, and a co-founder of Foresight Diagnostics.Head-to-head with PETThere are many hurdles for a diagnostic test between bench and bedside. “Before we operationalize a test like this, we need strong evidence to say the test is valid and performs better than PET,” says Herrera.Such evidence has been accumulating over the past 2-3 years. In August 2025, a coalition of researchers including Alizadeh published results comparing PhasED-Seq™ technology against PET scans as predictors of relapse in people treated for large B-cell lymphoma4.The team examined how ctDNA levels correlated with outcomes. People who went on to relapse had consistently higher levels of ctDNA, which was particularly pronounced at the end of treatment. “There's about a 30-fold difference in the likelihood of relapse between ctDNA-positive and -negative groups at the end of treatment," Alizadeh adds.What’s more, ctDNA analysis stratified patients independently of PET results. Of patients who had a positive PET result but no detectable ctDNA, only 7% went on to relapse; those who were PET-negative but ctDNA-positive had a 69% relapse rate. The test demonstrated 87% clinical sensitivity for detecting relapse and 92% clinical specificity for identifying durable remission.The ultra sensitivity of the phased-variant approach may also support earlier detection of cancer relapse during post-treatment surveillance. At ASH 2025, the annual meeting of the American Society of Hematology, Steven Wang of Amsterdam UMC and Stanford University presented a study of 166 patients in remission from large B-cell lymphoma5. He reported that, at the one-year post-treatment timepoint, a negative ctDNA-MRD test represented a 97% probability of remaining cancer free three years later, whereas a positive ctDNA-MRD result was associated with a 36% probability of relapse. Moreover, detecting ctDNA increased the likelihood of relapse in a time-dependent manner, suggesting that this type of test may support earlier relapse detection.Tests in clinicians’ handsInformed by achievements like these, the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) has updated its treatment guidelines for B-cell lymphoma to include the use of ctDNA analyses to confirm PET-positive scans, allowing for continued monitoring in the hopes of avoiding unnecessary treatment6.No guidance is given for the reverse scenario, but Alizadeh suggests: “For this group of patients, more robust data are needed to guide the selection between CAR-T therapies and bispecific antibodies.” Researchers are currently evaluating this suggestion to see if ultrasensitive ctDNA-MRD detection can guide treatment decisions (NCT06693830) and improve survival rates (NCT06500273).“In these studies, if a patient tests negative with PET but has detectable ctDNA at the end of therapy, they’re randomized to either get the standard of care (ongoing surveillance), or be pro-actively treated with consolidated CAR-T therapy,” explains Shouse, a principal investigator on the Alpha3 trial. “If the CAR-T therapy improves outcomes, it will be the first indication that this type of test can direct therapy in a meaningful way.”For now, the body of evidence suggests that ctDNA-MRD tests can at least provide more confidence for clinicians and peace of mind to patients who just want to know the status of their cancer.“Right now, it’s like a coin flip for physicians trying to decide if a patient still has cancer based on PET,” says Alizadeh. “ctDNA looks like it can do much better. We don’t get tests like this in oncology very often.”
Learn more about PhasED-SeqTM , which will be incorporated into Natera’s Signatera™ ctDNA-MRD detection test for lymphoma in 2026.
